*Old-Fashioned* (adj.)
A: of, relating to, or characteristic of a past era

replies.author: @Felice_Enellen
replies.text:
Bad take. The saying is that "money isn't _everything"_ for a reason. That's because it's acknowledging that money is _something_ in life. You need enough of it. It's just that once you get enough to be content, you realize (if you're not a fool) that chasing more is just a waste of your valuable time on earth and you're better off spending your time and energy on family & friends. 

It really irks me when people misinterpret sayings like this.

replies.author: @DarthZ01

replies.author: @Anonymous-ld7je
replies.text:
It's more that adding money to a crappy life won't magically make you happy. You'll just be a rich and lonely/miserable person, which is still a lot more comfortable than a poor and miserable person by the way.

But, if you otherwise have a good life, like a spouse you love, kids you love, hobbies/lifestyle you enjoy, then money buys a level of bliss on top of all that which is so intense it's almost hard to describe.

It buys the time and freedom to pursue who and what you love and life, and waste far less time on things you don't care about, like a dead end job for example.

replies.author: @FlamingCockatiel
replies.text: I firmly believe money can buy happiness. You never hear broke people saying it doesn't. I remember being a broke college  student and later tussling with turbulent employment, wondering how I was going to pay bills. Once I was paying off loans, I was definitely in a better mood.

replies.author: @Felice_Enellen
replies.text: @FlamingCockatiel It can only buy it temporarily. People quickly adapt to their new normal and become discontented again. There's always more stuff to buy. What you dream of owning today isn't enough tomorrow. The path to happiness is in wanting what you have instead of getting what you want. Once you get past the point of basic needs being met, you can stop just about any time if you only believe that to be true.

replies.author: @Felice_Enellen
replies.text:
​@southlondon86 I consider myself to be an example of what I say. I am not wealthy but I have enough money to feel secure in my basic needs. I am content. At one point I was offered a director-level position that would probably have doubled my salary and made my retirement fund even bigger, but would also have taken me away from the work I actually enjoyed doing and increased my stress levels & time away from home, family & friends. I declined. There is a crossover point between the quantity-of-money line and the quality-of-life line.

I also don't care about status. I grew up under the poverty line and I know that as long as you're fed and sheltered, you can _find_ or _make_ happiness even if you can't _buy_ it. Go watch the sunset/aurora/stars/clouds with your friend/lover, for instance. Go for walks. Explore nature if you can access it.

If anything, I usually feel like any financial disparity between me and someone else is shameful somehow on my part, so I don't brag and I try to help my less fortunate friends if they'll accept it, though most don't like feeling indebted even if you clearly state that something is a gift and there's no need to do anything in return or even mention it again.

I hope that answers your question. I don't actually like talking about myself like this, because this also feels like bragging, but you seem to be questioning whether or not I walk the talk, and I don't know how to respond otherwise.

replies.author: @Felice_Enellen
replies.text: ​@southlondon86 I tried to write something more detailed/nuanced but it keeps disappearing. Apparently I'm not allowed to speak at length. 😕 In short: not particularly. I have adequate money for my needs and sense of security. I would very likely have had significantly more by now if I'd taken the director-level position I was once offered, but that would have stressed me possibly into an early grave and kept me away from my family, so I chose to keep doing the work I actually loved for less money. I grew up under the poverty line and I know how to find happiness without ever-expanding material accumulation. Stuff that's as simple as a sunset with someone you love, or listening to the rain together. I hope this answers your question.

I do, however, point out that every time you do bark out ill-conceived, self-centered drivel that people like myself have every right to publicly display you for the arrogant, jealous, and conceited pile of crap you are.

replies.author: @Polynoe
replies.text:
@TechLeafRanger ​​⁠ I agree the movies butchered Ron’s character, rendering him more comic relief than anything else and often doing it to “improve” Hermione. This is not something she needed. Both are fantastic characters.

However, Hermione was absolutely right to go to McGonnagall about the Firebolt.

The whole series revolves around people attempting to murder Harry in various ways. In the books, wizards routinely use normally safe items to hurt one another. Not six months before Harry received the broom,  a 50-year-old diary possessed Ginny and unleashed a basilisk that nearly killed Hermione herself. They are thirteen-year-olds who know only a scrap of what’s possible.

An anonymous person sent Harry the most expensive broom on the market. A man everyone believes to be a killer is loose after an unprecedented, unexplained escape from Azkaban. He has already entered the castle by means no one but Lupin—who isn’t talking—knows. Going to McGonnagall made sense.

replies.author: @TechLeafRanger
replies.text:
​​@PolynoeIt would make sense of Harry had refused to listen to her after she voiced her concerns.
She did not beyond saying "I don't think anyone should ride that broom". She should have, afterwards, pulled Harry aside, talked to him, and expressed her thoughts and concerns.
She does not. Instead of trusting Harry to perhaps ACTUALLY listen to her, she sulks until it's time to eat, then runs off to McGonagall BEHIND Harry's back. 
What kind of friend does that? What friend doesn't trust another to try and talk to them first, and only going behind their back when they won't listen? The kind of friend who doesn't trust you, and if so, can you really call them a friend?
THAT is the problem: the fact that Hermione proves unwilling to trust that Harry will listen to her if she tries again, or at all.
Let's not forget she also blindly trusts Dumbledore to leave, with his abusive relatives, a TRAUMATIZED 15 YEAR OLD who just saw a classmate ended and the man that wants to end him revived, and have almost ZERO CONTACT that might help him deal with at least some of his trauma. Instead of actually helping her friend, like she should.
Ron doesn't do much better, but my point stands, I think. Not to mention her going behind his back with gathering people for the future DA, only to flip flop on her idea the instant she thinks Sirius would approve because Harry mentions it.

Jan 18, 2022 9:33am PT
Rupert Grint Views J.K. Rowling as ‘My Auntie’ Even if He Disagrees With Her: ‘It’s a Tricky One’ :

Rupert Grint recently told London’s The Times that he likens his relationship to “Harry Potter” author J.K. Rowling to that of an “auntie,” saying, “I don’t necessarily agree with everything my auntie says, but she’s still my auntie. It’s a tricky one.” 

Rowling has been the subject of controversy due to her controversial gender beliefs and a series of anti-transgender tweets posted in 2020.

replies.author: @rhiannonmcdaniel2313
replies.text:
​@bekker3544 Those are interesting theories! My theory is that actors who play the villain are both 1) not praised nearly as much as the actors of "good" characters and 2) activity boo-ed and disliked by their fans (who can't separate the actor from the character they play). This causes them to stay grounded and down-to-earth, which in turn causes them to not become nearly as arrogant and self-important as their good-character counterparts. 

I can imagine that for the actors who play good characters, the constant and overwhelming praise goes to their heads. It's generally unavailable due to human nature. But the actors who play bad characters don't have nearly as much of that being thrown at them constantly. This provides them the chance to stay grounded in reality. 

That's my theory, anyway.

What people believe has always been a private matter for most, it wasn't worn so openly before to immediately cause disagreements before anyone even has a chance to get to know each other before getting into personal matters. The exception to this are things like religions, in which, one is a norm typically and thus, is just a given most of the time if you're part of a majority religious group in a country. 

Social media was a mistake, to where these matters that used to be more private, are now worn as core identity traits instead of the interests, hobbies and humors that used to occupy those spaces in our interactions. Nowadays, people are just walking opinions and 'slactivists' that have romanticized being 'opposition' and never developed beyond being that, such as people who literally travel from state to state protesting as 'professional' protestors typically funded by third parties. Collecting other people's ideas while not being able to articulate any of them, are what these people become, and it shouldn't be this way. They should have other interests that people come to know them by LONG before these matters even surface, and once they do surface, they should have the emotional and intellectual maturity to articulate them in a  thoughtful, nonabrasive manner because there should be a mutual respect already established before these kinds of discussions even happen.

replies.author: @Norm-r7y
replies.text:
It takes a lot of courage to not fall blindly into lockstep with the party line on everything. The response from the party tells us a lot about how they handle different opinions. Republicans aren't perfect on this issue, but we've seen what happens to Democrats who step even one toe out of line. J.K. was openly vocal on one issue and now there are hundreds  (if not thousands) of people calling hor her to meet the same fate as Charlie Kirk.

 Here's my opinion: You can say whatever you want and believe whatever you want. I may not agree with you, but that doesn't make you a bad person and it sure doesn't mean that you deserve to be hurt in any way. But when you say something and people are calling for you to be harmed because of what you said, I'll never support anyone who calls for you to be harmed, even if I disagree with what you said. People need to wake up and understand that trying to silence those who disagree with you,  by any and all means necessary, is straight out of the Fascist playbook. The People who say that those who disagree with them deserve to d!e are the real enemies. Those who won't debate those that have opposing views and stand on the attitude of I'm right and anyone who disagrees with me is an idiot present a clear and present danger to one of the freedoms guaranteed to Americans. This has to stop. If we can't sit down and discuss issues calmly and rationally then we're in big trouble.

replies.author: @ickster23
replies.text: ​​@comedicsociopathyI respectfully disagree. First off, it is gender specific. Second is how you choose to calculate "middle age". You could take the life expectancy average, simply devide by two, declare you are the expert, and call others dumb. Similarly, one could look at the age where mortality rates climb significantly and use that as the starting point for calculating "middle age". For my own example, in Canada, close to 50 percent of males are dead by age 70, making 35 a far more realistic number for "middle age". Personally, I calculate a Canadian male to be middle aged between 35 and 41 years old. This secondary number is half of the average life expectancy according to stays Canada.

replies.author: @Malko_pile
replies.text:
@comedicsociopathy
If you live in a country where the average death age for a male is 70, then absolutely 35 is middle aged. 
This is math.
If the average death age is 80, then 40 is indeed middle aged.
Where you live matters.
For example, in Russia the death age is 68, so a middle aged Russian male is 34.

replies.author: @kennethfharkin
replies.text:
@Slo7hfulAcedia Horse droppings.  If it is so easy to "rip off" Grimm and others to make a series of books which millions fall in love with and spaws a massive movie franchise then you go do it.  Every storyteller has taken elements from those before.  Homer didn't come up with the Oluympian gods but he, or whoever it was, dropped them into the Illiad and the Odyssey along with others of their own creation, JUST AS ROWLINGS DID.  The woman was desitute and came up with a phenomenal litterary world, what have you done?  

In addition those young actors and actresses 100% owe their fame to being given parts in the stories she created.  No doubt about it.  They did not come into that franchise with any fame or recognition.  They certainly had to go through an audition process but that did not assure them fame.  Fame only came because they were attached to a phenomenally succesful franchise.  Any other actor who made it through the audition process to fill that role would have earned the same fame simply by being there and they should thank her every day for the lives they now have.  ANy other nameless child actor deemed capable of filling the role, and there were absolutely many of them, would have gained the same fame.  Yes, they did a good job in the role but none of them game in a performance like Ledger in Dark Knight, Klimer in Tombstone, Pacino in Devil's Advocate, or Duval in anything he showed up in.  That really none of them have done a damn thing other than Radcliff in the years since is pretty damn telling that it was the role, not the actor which brough the fame.  The only thing I even remebered her from other than Potter was a brief scene swinging a fire axe in This Is The End, and that was a joke role as herself with a callback to her as Hermione.

replies.author: @cosmicprison9819
replies.text:
1) Rowling thought all of the main actors were too attractive, that the characters were supposed to be geeks. She explicitly said so during a meet-up with Daniel Radcliffe. Daniel asked her if she didn’t expect that to happen during casting; Rowling replied “I’m not an idiot”.

2) Wesley Crusher was, as far as I know, accused of being Gene Roddenberry’s self-insert to some extent. Roddenberry had the idea that Wesley was supposed to be some childhood prodigy like Mozart. I recall as much from the Making Of on one of the Star Trek: TNG DVD collections. The problem is, most writers tend to show that a character is “smart” by not making them smarter, but by making everyone around them dumber whenever the “smart” characters enters the scene. In other words, Wesley’s “smarts” came at Data’s and Geordi’s expense.

replies.author: @schwaaard
replies.text: @SWANSWAN-nc7ds He also keeps it out of his films. Scientology - however you view it - is a way of life, not simply one aspect of it that is scarcely relevant to most interactions and decisions. And yet I don't recall a whiff of it in his films. That's the key to separating the art from the artist: the artist must separate their art from their personal views and biases. They don't HAVE to, of course, but then they risk alienating segments of their audience. It's easier for independent artists (authors, musicians, painters, etc..,) because their decisions and art typically only impacts them. If they know their take on life makes them relatable to a niche audience, and they want to reach that audience anyway, more power to 'em. They also don't need to make as much money to keep the lights on, so they can afford to have a narrower reach. When it comes to projects with big budgets and dozens, if not hundreds of careers on the line, broader appeal becomes necessary. And the crazy thing is, "broader appeal" in this case simply means refraining from including transparently polarizing issues that don't really make sense as inclusions from either a narrative or entertainment standpoint anyway.

replies.author: @foxincrocsgaming
replies.text:
I mean, I loved the books when I was a kid but as an adult they don't really hold up to scrutiny, though I wouldn't necessarily say they are some affront to English literature either. The writing is fine, not amazing, but fine. The world-building has plenty of cracks in it as long as you are willing to actually look. It's overall just an alright book.

The true feat of the Harry Potter series isn't that it is a meticulously crafted story and world where you can see how every cog turns another. The feat is that it is just such a fun world to imagine yourself in as a young person, tied together with time-tested universal themes and the evergreen structure of the hero's journey. It's not that it's good writing, it's that it's good storytelling. You can let yourself swim in it.

replies.author: @kgoblin5084
replies.text:
​@justaguywhoissleepdeprived6381 Well, the root of the issue is that Harry Potter has developed a fanbase that consumes nothing else, and in part pushes other, better books out of the market. Economics isn't entirely a zero-sum game, but there are some finite limits - there are natural limits on time, budgets, & shelf-space that leads to other worthy books not getting a chance because people instead go with the safe-sell.
Which of course leads to the natural counter reaction of "hey, if you liked HP, you should really also read ABX, which is actually BETTER than HP ror XYZ reasons..."

Personally, as a kid who was churning through thick multi-hundred pagers before he graduated from elementary school, it was safe to say I was quite BEMUSED when my university peers started raving over the 2nd coming of the Sand Fairy.

I'll also observe that this isn't limited to Rowling/HP either... if you dig for it you can find similar responses from fantasy - genre readers/writers about Tolkien... And in video games similar sentiments from FPS fans re:  Call of Duty, or RPG fans re: Pokemon. Orvin anime, with shows like Sword Art Online or Naruto. 

Basically anything with a notably large yet annoyingly exclusive fan base, despite obvious flaws in the focus.

Get ready to be shocked. As of 2025, his school ranks dead last in virtually every category. Attendance has gotten worse, violence has increased, and students steal and sell the school issued laptops and tablets that are given to them for free.

Hard to believe that this issue wasn't magically solved by throwing a bunch of money at it.

replies.author: @josephleisses3997
replies.text:
@Richforce1 
If we look at pro wrestling, itself a form of acting, it's always the people that play the heels, or bad guys, that end up being the nicest people in the world, while those that play faces, or good guys, end up as some of the biggest jerks in the business.
People like Tajiri, Kane, Nakamura, Eddie Guerrero, Trish Stratus, Awesome Kong, and even Andre the Giant were (and are, for those still living) some of the kindest people you'd ever find, while no one has anything good to say about the likes of Hogan, Ultimate Warrior, Alberto Del Rio, Moolah, both Hardys at various points in their life, Undertaker in recent years, and the list goes on.
Sure there's outliers, like Bradshaw generally played a heel and no one likes him, and there's few people that have anything bad to say about Cena, but they tend to be outliers. In general, those that agree to play the villains in a story do it because A, it's their job and B, so the hero of the story can struggle and triumph.

The actress - who had almost given up on her film career when she landed Hollywood's most badass female role - believes anyone who is not a feminist is by default sexist.

"People always ask me, 'Are you a feminist?'" Gadot tells Rolling Stone, in a new interview out this month.

author: @JustAnotherAccount8
text:
The irony that she played Hermione when in the books, Hermione started an activist group that attempted to "free" the house elves working in the kitchens of hogwarts, only to find out that they don't want to be "free" because they're safe, well fed and have satisfying work (something they wouldn't have if Hermione had her way). Ironically after learning this, she doubles down as a moral authority and tried to enforce her views onto them. Though well intentioned, her arrogance in her own views, her virtue signalling and moral superiority ended up leading to nothing.
Who would have thought that the actress portraying this character would lead a very similar life.

edit: to the folks saying this is slavery, please do yourself a favour and research the actual definition of slavery before you inevitably reduce the severity of the practice. By your definition, if I did unpaid volunteer work, I would be a slave; if I donated blood voluntarily, It would be like taking blood from a slave. These house elves are not human, they aren't even real, they are fictional magical creatures with their own culture and beliefs, you cannot project your human culture and beliefs onto them like Hermione attempted to do (such would be considered neocolonialism). They are treated well, they refuse pay, and at any point they can leave.

replies.text: Actually that is exactly what slaves did say when freed by the English. An English guy was in a yacht in a bay in a large North African city when the slaves were freed. That night all the slaves went into mourning because they knew they would now starve to death because they were unemployed. Which did happen.

replies.author: @greypilgrim228
replies.text: Also I think it's worth pointing out that all the House Elves in the Hogwarts kitchens were already free, they just chose to work there because it provided work, a roof, plenty of food, warmth etc. and House Elves by the nature of their race (which has been bred in captivity as household slaves of wealthy families for generations) enjoy working, or at least don't know how to do anything else to keep themselves busy, so Dumbledore was being kind to them, they were free to leave and would likely have been given help to find somewhere else to go if they'd asked for it. Winky, Barty Crouch's ex House Elf felt incredible shame at being dismissed from his service, and having nowhere else to go but Hogwarts, despite how much Dobby tried to show her it was better. Hermione was just being a know-it-all thinking she was right and morally superior, again, a mirror image of Emma Watson actually.

replies.author: @ScottishGremlin
replies.text: @greypilgrim228I don’t give much of a crap about Emma Watson but what the hell is wrong with wanting SLAVERY to be made illegal?

replies.author: @greypilgrim228
replies.text: @brandonontama2415 No, it literally didn't. It showed that a race (not analogous to our world btw, thanks in advance), that was bred into slavery and kept as slaves for generations is a difficult thing to break. If we face so many problems today based on the trans atlantic slave trade from a few hundred years ago where the dominant race (humans) enslaved those of its own race (humans) from elsewhere in the world, how much harder do you think it is of a powerful magical race enslaving another, smaller, weaker race, and then that small race is kept like that for hundreds of years, likely since medieval times and before, so probably a good thousand years. Dumbledore found a way to help ex House Elves, gave them shelter, employment, food etc. and any help they could want, but based on how they'd been bred and raised many felt more comfortable working than doing nothing. It's important to ease these changes slowly so culture and public desire develops with it, but also to give aid to any of those who do wish it, rather than forcing massive cultural and economical, social and psychological changes like Hermione wished block headedly. We see the difference here with the British and the Americans. Britain took the slower method, paying off the slave owners and freeing slaves, a debt it only fully paid off in 2015, while America in the North tried to force the issue in the South, and created its own civil war which cost hundreds of thousands of lives, and is still a raw issue in the country to this day nearly 200 years later; in contrast virtually nobody cares in Britain except views and issues imported from America. We in Britain have our own issues, like the Windrush scandal etc., but nothing like America.

replies.author: @greypilgrim228
replies.text: @ScottishGremlin Nothing is wrong with that view, I never said it was. But going in block-headedly and trying to force the issue American style, rather than British style gently, is going to help no one.

Dumbledore hired Dobby and Winky, but we're never told about the other house elves, the fact they're mad at Hermione and had started avoiding the common room because she was trying to trick them into accepting clothes indicates that they're actually not free. They also all wore uniforms, whereas Dobby and Winky wore normal clothes, showing again that THEY were still free, but the other house elves were not. 

Hermione wasn't wrong in sentiment, just in execution. Dobby himself calls his pillowcase a mark of the House Elf Enslavement, just as a matter of fact. They are enslaved, this isn't made up or a point for discussion, the books outright say they are enslaved.

replies.author: @watchchimp3102
replies.text:
Wow, massive blind spot there😉
Don't you think the portrayal of the Fictional,  magical house elves might have in any way been influenced by the authors own views? 
I haven't read the books, likely never will. But your own description of them certainly fits the description  of what being a slave is. 
The idea that they were happy with their situation doesn't make them any less slaves and is the same sort of rationalizing that we see for real live slaves. 
If the house elves were happy in their roles than Dobby wouldn't have been so excited when he received a friggin sock. 
I'm reminded of the description of Harry Potter as "Star Wars with shittier light sabers" The parallels  are hard to ignore and make it kind of difficult for Rowling to accuse anyone else of being a grifter.

replies.author: @yingshenghuang3439
replies.text: Here's the definition of slavery: "Slavery is the ownership of a person as property, especially in regards to their labour". Now tell me how slavery is not implied when the owner practically owns house elves (obviously elves can't leave freely and are usually mistreated, as opposed to your belief), and such ownership is reflected in the way that elves must obey their order, whether they like it or not, and they can only be freed when the owner gives them clothes. Whether or not elves are voluntary is irrelevant, since there's such thing as voluntarily slavery. The problem is not whether they want to work, but rather if they can choose not to work. You doing unpaid work or donating blood voluntarily is not slavery, but if you are compelled to donate blood and to work, that's borderline slavery.

The house elves at Hogwarts were well looked after, but house elves like Dobby definitely weren’t. While Hermione was initially flawed at first by focusing on Hogwarts house elves, she later adjusted her view (when she became minister for magic)
House elves WERE treated as slaves, physically mentally and emotionally abused, and were not considered living beings. Winky, Dobby and the Black Family’s previous house elves are proof of that.
The examples you give in your edit aren’t really relevant either. House elves are trapped and aren’t allowed freedom unless given clothes by their masters. You actively choose to donate blood and volunteer, and you’re free to leave. House elves aren’t. 
I get what you’re saying though. Hermione was extremely self righteous and arrogant at times, especially during her interactions with Luna.

replies.text: A big-shot writer like JK Rowling has a massive megaphone—millions of fans, a killer storytelling knack, and a platform like X that blasts her words to the world. She drops a raw, heartfelt thread calling out hypocrisy (say, Emma Watson's flip-flop), and boom: it goes viral, memes explode, doubters nod along, and suddenly the crowd flips from "JKR's the bad guy" to "Wait, she's got a point." It's like rewriting the plot mid-book—her voice reshapes the "story" everyone tells about the drama, turning underdog grit into the new hero vibe. Simple as that: words from the right pen? They rewrite reality.

replies.text:
So, it's perhaps not surprising that Gal Gadot has strong opinions on the topic of feminism.

The actress - who had almost given up on her film career when she landed Hollywood's most badass female role - believes anyone who is not a feminist is by default sexist.

"People always ask me, 'Are you a feminist?'" Gadot tells Rolling Stone, in a new interview out this month.

"And I find the question surprising, because I think, 'Yes, of course. Every woman, every man, everyone should be a feminist. Because whoever is not a feminist is a sexist.'"
